what: what is the constructor method in a class
why: why use a constructor in a class
output: must call super constructor in derived class

1_beginner_explanation.summary: Inside every `class`, there is a special method called `constructor()`. This is the exact function that runs the moment you use the `new` keyword to create an instance. It is used to set up the initial data (properties) for the object before it is handed back to you.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: If the class is a car factory, the `constructor()` is the specific assembly worker whose job is to paint the car and install the custom seats based on your specific order details before the car leaves the factory.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: When `new MyClass(args)` is called, the engine creates an empty object, binds it to `this`, and immediately passes the `args` into the `constructor(args)` method. If you don't write a constructor, JS silently injects an empty one: `constructor() {}`.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: The `constructor` method is the only part of the class that maps directly to the actual function object created in memory. All other methods go on the `.prototype`.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Accepting arguments when creating a new class instance.

4_common_confusion.trap: Trying to write `function constructor()` or `constructor: function()`. A class body is not a standard object literal. You don't use the `function` keyword, and you don't use commas.
4_common_confusion.fix: Just write `constructor() { ... }` directly inside the class brackets.

6_edge_case.quirk: A class can only have ONE `constructor()`. If you try to write multiple constructors (to handle different argument types, like in Java), JavaScript will throw a SyntaxError.
